text: The salt flats of Bolivia stretch flat and white in every direction, farther than the eye can resolve. The horizon disappears. Sky and ground become one pale continuous surface, broken only by distant mountains that float like dark islands. At midday the heat is absolute and the silence is physical. After rain, a thin sheet of water covers everything, turning the flats into a perfect mirror that reflects clouds with disturbing accuracy. You cannot tell which way is up. Tourists stand at the edge of this disorientation and photograph each other looking small. The scale erases ordinary proportion. You feel the planet's curvature not as a concept but as something you can actually see from where you stand.
